coredata.dc:title: Egg shell-derived calcium phosphate/carbon dot nanofibrous scaffolds for bone tissue engineering: Fabrication and characterization
coredata.dc:creator: Shafiei, Shervin
Omidi, Meisam
Nasehi, Fatemeh
Golzar, Hossein
Mohammadrezaei, Dorsa
Rezai Rad, Maryam
Khojasteh, Arash
coredata.dc:description: Recent exciting findings of the particular properties of Carbon dot (CDs) have shed light on potential biomedical applications of CDs-containing composites. While CDs so far have been widely used as biosensors and bioimaging agents, in the present study for the first time, we evaluate the osteoconductivity of CDs in poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [PCL/PVA] nanofibrous scaffolds. Moreover, further studies were performed to evaluate egg shell-derived calcium phosphate (TCP3) and its cellular responses, biocompatibility and in vitro osteogenesis. Scaffolds were fabricated by simultaneous electrospinning of PCL with three different types of calcium phosphate, PVA and CDs. Fabricated scaffolds were characterized by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), contact angle measurement and degradation assessment. SEM, the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay, and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity test were performed to evaluate cell morphology, proliferation and osteogenic differentiation, respectively. The results demonstrated that while the addition of just 1 wt% CDs and TCP3 individually into PCL/PVA nanocomposite enhanced ALP activity and cell proliferation rate (p < 0.05), the synergetic effect of CDs/TCP3 led to highest osteogenic differentiation and proliferation rate compared to other scaffolds (p < 0.05). Hence, CDs and PCL/PVA-TCP3 could serve as a potential candidate for bone tissue regeneration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Egg shell-derived calcium phosphate
Bone regeneration

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Research Deputy, Dental School, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences and Department of Tissue Engineering, School of Advanced Technologies in Medicine, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences , Tehran, Iran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b) TEM images of fabricated carbon dots at different magnifications, (c) UV–Visible absorption spectrum of carbon dots solution, (d) photoluminescence response of carbon dots solution at various excitation wavelengths.
(a) Overall XPS survey of carbon dots, high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.
SEM images of different nanofibrous scaffolds: PCL/PVA (A), PCL/PVA-TCP1 (B), PCL/PVA-TCP2 (C), PCL/PVA-TCP3 (D), PCL/PVA-CD (E), PCL/PVA-TCP1-CD (F), PCL/PVA-TCP2-CD (G) and PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD (H).

(a) Degradation rate of different scaffolds at different time points during 56 days, (b) FTIR spectra of PCL/PVA, CD, PCL/PVA-TCP1-CD, PCL/PVA-TCP2-CD, PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD, (c) XRD patterns of TCP1, TCP2, TCP3.

SEM images of hBFPSCs cultured on different nanofibrous scaffolds after 5 days in growth medium: PCL/PVA (a), PCL/PVA-TCP1 (b), PCL/PVA-TCP2 (c), PCL/PVA-TCP3 (d), PCL/PVA-CD (e), PCL/PVA-TCP1-CD (f), PCL/PVA-TCP2-CD (g), and PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD (h).

(a) Proliferation assessment of hBFPSCs cultured on different nanofibrous scaffolds by MTT assay after 1, 3, and 5 days in growth medium. Please note that the tissue culture plate used as control (significant differences at p < 0.05), (b) DAPI staining images of hBFPSCs cultured on different nanofibrous scaffolds after 5 days in growth medium: PCL/PVA (A), PCL/PVA-TCP1 (B), PCL/PVA-TCP2 (C), PCL/PVA-TCP3 (D), PCL/PVA-CD (E), PCL/PVA-TCP1-CD (F), PCL/PVA-TCP2-CD (G), and PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD (H).
Osteogenic capability of hBFPSCs cultured on different nanofibrous scaffolds by ALP activity assay after 7 and 14 days in (a) growth and (b) osteogenic medium (significant differences at p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Egg shell-derived calcium phosphate/carbon dot nanofibrous scaffolds for bone tissue engineering: Fabrication and characterization

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Advanced Technologies in Medicine
Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent exciting findings of the particular properties of Carbon dot (CDs) have shed light on potential biomedical applications of CDs-containing composites. While CDs so far have been widely used as biosensors and bioimaging agents, in the present study for the first time, we evaluate the osteoconductivity of CDs in poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [PCL/PVA] nanofibrous scaffolds. Moreover, further studies were performed to evaluate egg shell-derived calcium phosphate (TCP3) and its cellular responses, biocompatibility and in vitro osteogenesis. Scaffolds were fabricated by simultaneous electrospinning of PCL with three different types of calcium phosphate, PVA and CDs. Fabricated scaffolds were characterized by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), contact angle measurement and degradation assessment. SEM, the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay, and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity test were performed to evaluate cell morphology, proliferation and osteogenic differentiation, respectively. The results demonstrated that while the addition of just 1 wt% CDs and TCP3 individually into PCL/PVA nanocomposite enhanced ALP activity and cell proliferation rate (p < 0.05), the synergetic effect of CDs/TCP3 led to highest osteogenic differentiation and proliferation rate compared to other scaffolds (p < 0.05). Hence, CDs and PCL/PVA-TCP3 could serve as a potential candidate for bone tissue regeneration.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrospun nanofiberous scaffolds have gained growing interest due to the structural similarity to the bone extracellular matrix (ECM) [1–3]. This similarity provides proper support for cells and guides their behaviors such as attachment, proliferation and differentiation [4]. Between various synthetic polymers, which have been successfully electrospun into nanofibers, Poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL) has attracted more attention due to its biodegradability, biocompatibility, relatively low melting point (55–60 °C) and good blend-compatibility with other additives [5–7]. However, some shortcomings including low degradation time and lack of bioactivity, hamper its application in bone tissue engineering [8–10].

To overcome these issues, Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA), which possesses proper biocompatibility, biodegradability, and excellent mechanical properties can be considered as a potential additive [11]. Hence, the blend of PCL/PVA has been used to fabricate a bioscaffold with proper physical and chemical properties [12,13].

Moreover, because the ECM of native bone composed of both organic and inorganic nanofibers [1], combining polymers with bioactive inorganic materials, such as β-tricalcium phosphate (β–TCP) [14,15] via co-electrospinning considered a fascinating approach in order to design a scaffold with appropriate properties for bone tissue engineering [16,17]. The high cost of commercially available TCPs can restrict their use, so that natural compounds such as egg-shell may use as a source of TCPs due to their biological origin and low cost [18,19]. Although they are occasionally used as a fertilizer due to their high content of calcium and nitrogen [20], most of them are discarded as waste. Their porous structure, optimal stability at high temperatures, and their biological properties such as enhancing osteoblast adhesion compared to synthetic calcium phosphate are among the advantages that TCPs synthesized by egg-shell have been used for bone tissue engineering [21–24].

Moreover, to improve the differentiation of stem cells into osteoblast lineage, carbon nanostructure materials can play a significant role due to their osteoinductive, physicochemical and biomimetic properties [25–27]. Among various carbon-based nanostructures, such as graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes, carbon dots (CDs), with their zero dimensions, are widely used as biosensors and bioimaging agents because of their optimal properties such as low toxicity, water solubility, functionalization, and photostability [28–30]. Recently, great efforts have been paid to their potential applications of CDs in drug and gene delivery, and other aspects of biomedical applications [31,32]. Excellent electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission property of CDs may also use for monitoring the healing process in real-time manner. Geng et al. [33] reported the higher expression of alkaline phosphate, extracellular matrix, osteopontin and osteocalcin in rat Bone Mesenchymal Stem Cells using Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots also used with hydroxyapatite nanoparticles and enhancement in alkaline phosphatase activity, mineralization, and expression of the osteogenic genes was seen in osteoblast cells in a zebrafish jawbone regeneration model [34]. To the best of our knowledge, there is a lack of study on the potential of CDs for osteogenic differentiation in bone tissue engineering. In this study, we fabricated biocomposite electrospun nanofibrous scaffolds using PCL/PVA/eggshell-derived TCP, and CDs and to confirm the applicability of this bioscaffold for bone tissue engineering, after physical characterizations, further biological assessments including proliferation and osteogenic differentiation were carried out using human buccal fat pad-derived stem cells (hBFPSCs).

CDs were synthesized using one pot hydrothermal reaction, as described in previous literatures [32]. In brief, ammonium hydrogen citrate (1 g) was dissolved in deionized water (40 mL) to achieve a clear solution. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-still autoclave reactor (50 mL) and then, heated up to 200 °C for 12 h. After completion of the reaction, the vessel was allowed to naturally cool down to obtain a bright yellow solution, indicating the formation of carbon dots. The solution was centrifuged (12,000 rpm for 20 min) to remove the agglomerated and large particles. Finally, for further purification of the resulting solution, the solution was filtered using a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) in 2 L of deionized water for 24 h.

The Gallus egg-shells were collected and rinsed in acetic acid (5%), ethanol (80%) and deionized water, respectively. After cleaning, the egg-shells were ground using a ball and mill. Then, the powder was sieved using a 100 μm strainer. The powder was reacted with orthophosphoric acid (weight ratio = 1:5) for 24 h. The resulting slurry was dried at 100 °C for 12 h which is referred to as TCP1 in this paper. Subsequently, TCP1 was sintered at 800 °C for 4 h to obtain TCP3. In addition, Commercial available TCP was purchased from Merck (Germany) referred to as TCP2.

In this study, 12% w/v PCL solution was prepared by dissolving PCL (80.000 MW, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, United States) in admixture of dimethylformamide and chloroform. Next, 10% w/v PVA solution was prepared by dissolving PVA (30.000–70.000 MW, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, United States) in deionized water. Afterwards, PCL and PVA solutions were separately poured into 5 mL plastic syringes with 20-gauge steel needles and loaded in the electrospinning syringe pump system. Subsequently, the resulting solution was electrospun at a voltage of 18 kV and rotating aluminum foil collector operating at 300 rpm (Nanoazma, Tehran, Iran). The flow rates were 0.5 mL/h and 0.3 mL/h for PCL and PVA, respectively. The distance between the syringe needle tip and the collecting rotating mandrel was 15 cm. The electrospinning process was performed at room temperature (25 °C). Two syringe pumps were used simultaneously for each of the PCL and PVA solutions to fabricate randomly aligned separate PCL and PVA nanofibers scaffolds.

Then, 1% TCP solution was dissolved in DMF and CHO in a sonic mixer for 2 h prior to the addition of PCL. After that, 1% TCP1, TCP2 and TCP3 were added to the PCL solution and electrospun with pure PVA to fabricate PCL/PVA-TCP1, PCL/PVA-TCP2, and PCL/PVA-TCP3 scaffolds, respectively. For the next 4 scaffolds, 1% CD was added to the PVA solutions for electrospinning and PCL/PVA-CD, PCL/PVA-TCP1-CD, PCL/PVA-TCP2-CD, and PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD were fabricated in order to compare with the no-CD scaffolds.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips, EM 208) images were obtained to evaluate the morphology and size of agglomerated CDs. The UV–visible spectrum (Rey Leigh UV-2601 spectrometer) of CD solutions and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy scanning (Avaspec 2048 TEC, Avantes) at various excitation wavelengths from 220 to 400 nm were performed for structural analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Bestec, Germany) was used to evaluate the chemical composition of as-prepared CD solutions.

The morphological characteristics of different nanofiber scaffolds were assessed by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU3500, Japan) after mounting of samples on aluminum stubs and their coating with a thin layer of gold. For each scaffold, SEM micrographs were taken at 1.00, 3.00, 5.00, and 10.00× magnifications for accurate analysis. The mean fiber diameter of scaffolds was determined by measuring 15 fibers per sample on SEM micrographs.
FTIR was performed to identify and compare the composition of scaffolds and determine the TCP and CD groups in the composite scaffolds (Bruker, USA).

The composition of all TCP powders and their crystalline phases was characterized and compared using X-Ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD, Bruker, UK) with Cu target (λ = 1.54060) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The start, end and step diffraction angles (2 theta) were 10.000, 80.020, and 0.060, respectively.

The water contact angle analysis was measured by dropping water on a random area on the surface of scaffold at room temperature (25 °C). The contact angles were evaluated by contact angle goniometer (Dataphysics, CA 15 plus), and all scaffolds were tested in duplicate.

The degradation rate of different nanofiber scaffolds was determined by the percentage of mass loss in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, Gibco by Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA). Each scaffold was weighed with a laboratory scale (Sartorius, USA) on day 0 and then soaked in PBS for 56 days. At 1, 7, 14, 18, 21, and 56 days, the scaffolds were rinsed with deionized water and dried for 7 days (n = 3), followed by weighing each scaffolds to calculate their mass loss.

The hBFPSCs were obtained from the cell bank of School of Advanced Technologies in Medicine of Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences. The cells were re-suspended in stem cell growth medium containing Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM; Gibco by Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA), 15% fetal bovine serum (FBS; Gibco by Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin (10,000 U/mL; Gibco by Life Technologies, Carlsbad, CA, USA). The medium was changed every 4 days. Third and fourth passage cells were used for this subsequent analysis.

Scaffolds with the size of 7 × 7 × 7 mm were used for in vitro studies. A total of 10,000 hBFPSCs were seeded onto each scaffold in 48-well plates. The loaded scaffolds were then incubated in stem cell growth medium.

Cell attachment and morphology were evaluated after 3 and 5 days of incubation in growth medium by SEM. The scaffolds were prepared for SEM assessment as follows: They were first fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde, dehydrated using graded ethanol. Then, they were vacuum-dried, and sputter-coated with gold. Samples were examined using a SEM (Hitachi SU3500, Japan).

Cell proliferation on different scaffolds was evaluated using the colorimetric methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, United States). For this purpose, the samples (after 2, 4, and 6 days of cell seeding) were rinsed with PBS to remove unattached cells and incubated with 200 mL of MTT solution (5 mg mL−1 in DMEM without FBS) at 37 °C for 3 h. The supernatant was removed and a constant amount of dimethyl sulfoxide solvent (DMSO, Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, United States) was added to each sample. The incubated suspension was then pipetted into a 96-well plate, and the absorbance was read at 570 nm using an ELISA reader (ELX800; BioTeK, Winooski, VT, USA). The same procedure was performed for the cultured cells in tissue culture plate as control.

Also, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI, Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, USA) staining was performed to evaluate the proliferation, spreading and adhesion of cultured hBFPSCs on different nanofibrous scaffolds. To do this, 100 μl of 0.1 μg/mL of DAPI was used to stain the nuclei for 15 min which is followed by 3 times PBS washing. Subsequently, cells were mounted in medium for 24 h at 25 °C to investigate their proliferation using confocal microcopy (Prairie Technologies, Inc.).

The scaffolds were incubated in stem cell growth medium for 1 day; then, they were immersed in osteogenic medium for 7 and 14 days. The scaffolds were washed twice with chilled PBS. Then, they were lysed using Celytic cell lysis reagent (Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, USA) for 30 min. The cell lysate was mixed with p-nitrophenol phosphate substrate solution (Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, Missouri, USA) and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Next, 3 N NaOH was used to stop the reaction. The absorbance was read at 405 nm using ELISA reader (BioTek, Winooski, VT, USA).

Quantitative results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was carried out using one-way ANOVA, post hoc multiple comparisons test, Bonferroni pairwise comparison test and nonparametric tests. p ˂ 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The shape, size, and physical properties of CDs were characterized using TEM, dynamic light scattering (DLS), UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (Fig. 1 ). As can be seen in Fig. 1a and b, carbon dots showed a uniform size distribution without any significant agglomeration which may occur during the CDs synthesis. Moreover, DLS analysis showed that the mean size of the CDs was about 6 ± 1 nm which is in a good agreement with TEM images. Fig. 1c shows the UV–visible spectrum of CD solutions indicating two major absorption peaks and a tail extending to the visible region. The distinctive absorption peak at 220 and 335 nm correspond to π–π* and n–π* transition of CO bond, respectively [35]. The excitation dependence of carbon dots was evaluated using PL spectroscopy scanning at various excitation wavelengths from 220 to 400 nm. As presented in Fig. 1d, the as-prepared CDs revealed an excitation-independent behavior owing to unchanged emission peak by varying the excitation wavelength. Accordingly, all the PL spectra revealed emission wavelength at 463 ± 5 nm suggesting uniform size distribution of CD nanoparticles, which also confirmed by TEM images [36].

The chemical composition of CD solutions was investigated using XPS spectroscopy (Fig. 2 ). The overall XPS spectrum revealed the presence of three major peaks, composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in the CDs (Fig. 2a). As seen in Fig. 2b, the high-resolution spectrum of CDs was divided into four major peaks indicating the possible chemical bonds of carbon atoms. The major peak in the expanded XPS spectrum was related to CC bonds at 284.8 eV (18). The distinctive peaks at 285.4, 287.8, and 289.3 eV shown in Fig. 2b could be attributed to CN, CO, and COC bonds, respectively [37]. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of N1s indicates two characteristic peaks at 400.2 and 401.5 eV, assigned to CN and NH bonds, respectively [38] (Fig. 2c). Also, the distinctive O1s peaks at 531.6 and 533.1 eV shown in Fig. 2d were associated with CO and COH or COC bonds, respectively [38]. Based on these results, it can be concluded that the nitrogen and oxygen atoms were successfully doped in carbon dots.

As hydrophilic surfaces can lead cells to grow and adhere better by providing a proper condition for the attachment of adhesive proteins such as fibronectin and vitronectin [43,44], the contact angel (CA) value of the fabricated scaffolds was measured and tabulated (Table 1). As can be seen in Table 1, all scaffolds possess a hydrophilic surface and there is no significant reduction in CA values after adding TCPs and CDs compared with PCL/PVA; however, the PCL/PVA-CD had a slightly lower contact angle (29°) compared with other scaffolds. Indeed, high wettability in all of our fabricated scaffolds with or without TCP showed that although polymeric scaffolds such as PCL have a hydrophobic property which is unfavorable for cellular attachment [40], adding hydrophilic water-soluble synthetic polymer such as PVA and minerals such as HA may significantly improve the hydrophilicity [45]. It is interesting to note that the addition of β-TCP can individually increase the wettability of the scaffold but PVA probably masked the hydrophilic potency of other additives such as TCP. We assumed that as same as this logic might be happened in a case of CD addition. Ma et al. [46] investigated the effect of adding different mass ratio of β-TCP to untreated or modified β-TCP/PLLA composites and reported that the hydrophilicity increased by adding 10 wt% β-TCP while increasing the mass ratio of β-TCP to 20 wt% dramatically decreased the CA value.

Moreover, one of the main challenges in the application of PCL is attributed to its low degradation time; hence enhancing the hydrophilicity of the scaffold could be an efficient way to increase the biodegradability of the fabricated scaffold due to the fact that higher hydrolytic reactions lead to favorably higher biodegradability [47,48].

During the degradation process, some beneficial physicochemical properties of the scaffolds may be lost by polymer mass reduction which can occur via various mechanisms including solvation or depolymerization [49]. Hence, achieving an appropriate degradation rate can be considered as a key factor in the scaffold fabrication process. The degradation rate of scaffolds was quantitatively analyzed over 56 days using the PBS immersion method (Fig. 4a). As can be seen in Fig. 4a, not only all scaffolds show over 20% of weight loss in PBS solution on day 56 with no significant difference between groups at all given time points but it can be also concluded that the addition of TCPs and CDs slightly decreases the weight loss of the scaffolds. This may be attributed to the higher density of the interaction between biomaterials and scaffold [50].

It is interesting to note that of all scaffolds, the PCL/PVA-TCP1-CD had a lower weight loss which can be rooted in its crystalline structure which results in different absorption characteristics [51,52].

Moreover, despite the characteristic features of PCL polymeric-based scaffolds including slow rate of degradation in vivo [40], the higher degradability and solubility of β-TCP compared with other bioceramics such as HA can comprehensibly explain the accelerated degradation of PCL-based nanofibers, however; no significant difference was observed among of the scaffolds [53,54].

The formation of intertwined network between different TCPs and polymer chains was examined by using FTIR analysis (Fig. 4b). The FTIR spectrum of CD exhibited characteristic peaks at 1650 and 3300 cm−1 attributed to CC and OH bonds, respectively [54]. With regards to PCL/PVA, there is a broad band between 3200 and 3500 cm−1 which can be assigned to OH stretch from inter/intramolecular hydrogen bonds [49]. In addition, the peaks at 1172, 1250, 1731, 2869 and 2941 correspond to CO, CO stretching in ester group, CO, asymmetric and symmetric CH2 stretching, respectively [39,55]. When it comes to the FTIR spectra of the scaffolds containing TCPs, not only all these characteristic peaks still remain, but some new distinctive peaks also emerge between 900 and 1200 cm−1 which are represented three-fold degenerate stretching mode of PO4 −3 [56,57]. More specifically, the peaks around 725 cm−1 in TCP3 seemed to be characteristic for the calcium pyrophosphate phase. The peak at 940–970 cm−1 in TCP1, with a sharper band in TCP3, showed the symmetric stretching mode of PO4 −3 in the β-TCP structure [57]. Sharp peaks showed that the eggshell-derived TCPs were well crystallized and similar to the standard pattern of β-TCP.

The sintering process of TCP1 led to the formation of β-TCP which can readily confirmed by comparing the XRD patters of different TCPs (Fig. 4c). As it can be seen in Fig. 4c, the XRD pattern of TCP1 showed characteristic peaks of α-TCP which were similar with the pattern reported by Kolmas et al. [58]. Moreover, heated eggshell-derived TCP and TCP3 illustrated approximately the similar pattern which can readily confirm the formation of β-TCP-like structure in TCP3 [59]. In addition, slight reduction in peak density showed multiple crystalline phases in sintered TCP group. Similar results were also reported by Lee et al. [60].

The first step in cells culture on the scaffold surface is attachment and spreading which plays a key role in modulating the forthcoming cell behaviors, including cell proliferation and cell differentiation [61]. As can be seen in Fig. 5 , fabricated scaffolds represented promising structures for cell adhesion and spreading, as hBFPSCs were completely attached to the entire length of the fibers in all types of scaffolds which can be attributed to the high surface wettability and surface roughness of the composites [61].

Surface roughness is a vital factor influencing biological activity of biomaterials. In general, higher roughness can provide more surface areas for interaction with adhesive protein molecules and cells, which facilitate cell attachment and growth [62,63]. In a study done by Kaur T et.al [64], it has been found that surface roughness of carbon-based materials as well as van der Waals and electrostatic forces between functional groups of them and the functional groups of the adhesive protein can lead to high protein adsorption and promoted cell adhesion. Moreover, nanofibrous structure and increased surface area and roughness due to the addition of minerals such a TCP and HA could enhance cell adhesion and spreading on polymeric-based scaffolds [42].

Additionally, CA value of all scaffolds was below 45° (Table 1) which is an appropriate representative of a high surface hydrophilicity of them [65]. It has been proved that hydrophilic surfaces have more biocompatibility and provide a better condition for cells to attach and grow compared to hydrophobic surfaces [43]. Hence, proper cell attachment and spreading confirmed by SEM and nuclear staining showed favorable biocompatibility of PCL/PVA-based scaffolds with different types of TCP and CDs in our study, which was in agreement with previous findings [40].

Studies have exhibited that cells changed their morphology which results in binding cells to the biomaterial surfaces, in response to their microenvironments [66]. Indeed, Surface morphology of substrates can affect the cell morphology, and then cell morphology can influence the cell functions including cell adhesion, proliferation and particularly differentiation [8,67].

As can be observed in Fig. 5, after 5 days, cells showed elongation and spindle-shaped (fibroblast-like) morphology with multiple processes in orientation of fibers. Indeed, hydrophilic surface could contribute to better cell attachment and elongation [68]. Elongated cells with multi-branched morphology are an early indicator of osteogenic differentiation [69]. For instance, Kumar S et.al represented that cells cultured on the PCL/GO, PCL/AGO [70] and PCL/GO-PEI [71] became spindle-shaped, elongated and branched, which have improved potential for better cell attachment, proliferation and osteogenic differentiation. This is due to the existence of hydrophilic structure of GO and AGO, whereas cells grown on PCL/RGO showed rounded morphology. Indeed, chemical composition and matrix morphology on a nanometric scale both play a critical role in cell-scaffold interactions as an initial phase in osteogenic differentiation [72].

Fig. 6a shows the cell viability and proliferation of hBFPSCs on different scaffolds using the MTT assay, at 1, 3, and 5 days. With increasing time from day 1 to 5, the cell viability of all samples significantly increased. At the first day, PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD showed a slightly lower, but not significant, proliferation rate compared with other samples. However, addition of TCP3 and CDs after 5 days, dramatically enhance the cell viability and proliferation compared to other groups. Cell attachment, proliferation, and spreading were also confirmed with nuclear staining method using DAPI staining (Fig. 6b).

Many cytotoxicity studies have represented that CDs show very low toxicity and can be easily applied to the biomedical application [73]. Gogoi et al. [74] illustrated that the incorporation of CDs in the polyurethane membrane led to better cell proliferation.

Oxidative stress is the major cytotoxic mechanism [75] and different cell lines exhibit different antioxidative capacity [76]. Low reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels can be adjusted by normal cell functions. While high ROS production leads to oxidative damage of cells and death [77]. Havrdova et al. [78] examined the cytotoxicity effects of CDs on different types of cell line, at different doses and with various surface coverages. ROS formation was shown to slightly increase with an increment in CDs concentration. However, a remarkable increase happened for concentrations from 50 mg mL−1 up to 200 mg mL−1, leading to a rapid decline in cell viability. Moreover, Incorporation of TCP3 could enhance the compatibility of cells. This enhanced compatibility could also contribute to improvement of cells proliferation [79]. Similarly, Rodrigues et al. [80] showed a higher rate of proliferation by adding TCP to PCL nanofiber scaffolds. Ultimately, all the results showed that PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD have the very high biocompatibility due to the synergistic effect of heated TCP and CDs.

Fig. 7a and b show the results of ALP activity in both growth and osteogenic medium, respectively. The results of ALP activity in growth medium showed that all samples, excepting pure PCL/PVA, had a significantly higher ALP activity at 14 days compared to 0 and 7 days in growth medium and of all scaffolds, PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD exhibited the greatest ALP activity (p < 0.05). However, in osteogenic medium, the ALP activity of all samples significantly increased from day 0 to 7 (p = 0.000) and then slightly decreased on day 14, except in PCL/PVA-TCP2-CD and the control groups. Reduction in ALP activity on day 14 compared to day 7 in osteogenic medium was not significant in the samples. Pairwise comparison in the growth medium on day 14 revealed the significant higher of ALP activity in PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD and the control groups (p < 0.05). Interestingly, a significant lower ALP activity in PCL/PVA-TCP3 without CD compared with PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD and the control groups at this time point i.e., p = 0.009 and p = 0.007, respectively.

On the other hand, in an osteogenic medium, PCL/PVA-TCP1 had significantly (p = 0.028) higher ALP activity compared with PCL/PVA-TCP3 (day 7). Eventually, pairwise comparisons showed significantly higher ALP activity in scaffolds with CD compared with the scaffold without CD in the growth medium at 14 days (p = 0.035). In the osteogenic medium, the scaffold with CD had a slightly (but not significant) higher ALP activity on 14 days and a lower rate at 0 and 7 days.

The positive osteogenic effect of HA and TCP has been shown in various studies [40,81,82], but the osteoinductive capability of egg shell-derived TCPs investigated in a few studies [18,57]. Jayashree et al. reported the fabrication of tetra‑calcium phosphate by solid-state reaction method with eggshell source and compared it with tetra‑calcium phosphate prepared by using synthetic Ca(CO)3 [57]. The egg shell-derived tetra‑calcium phosphate fabricated by solid-state reaction method demonstrated a higher rate of cell proliferation and stability compared to the control group [57]. Prabhash Dadhich et al. also reported a higher rate of proliferation and differentiation activity in 3D printed multi-phasic calcium phosphate scaffold compared with the chemically synthesized type [83]. The possible factors that may contribute to higher rate of osteoactivity in natural-origin bioceramics especially eggshell-derived TCP include the presence of biologically relevant ions, the explicit crystal structure of natural origin calcium phosphate, biodegradation [10,57,83]. In vivo studies on eggshell-derived bioceramics such as HA also revealed higher bone formation compared to synthetic types [84].

Apart from the application of CDs in biomedicine such as drug delivery, intracellular bioimaging modalities, and biosensors for its multi stimulus responses [85], our findings also demonstrated osteoinductive properties of CDs in the field of bone tissue engineering. Gogoi reported biocompatibility of polyurethane/CD nanocomposites for proliferation and differentiation of MG 63 osteoblast cells [74]. Chandrani Sarkar et al. used CDs in the form of CDs decorated carboxymethyl cellulose hydroxyapatite nanocomposite to evaluate its applications such as osteogenic activity, bio-imaging and drug delivery. This study also indicated osteogenic properties of CDs in osteoblast-like MG63 cells [86]. Further in-depth in vivo studies are recommended to assess the efficacy of biocomposite scaffolds containing CDs as well as heated egg shell-derived TCPs in bone tissue engineering.

In summary, a novel scaffold containing heated egg shell-derived TCP and carbon dots were fabricated. The CDs were synthesized and characterized by using TEM, UV–visible spectrum, PL spectroscopy and XPS. Then, β-TCP was fabricated by heating egg shell derived TCP which its phase analysis was examined by XRD. After that, PCL/PVA-TCPs-CD scaffolds fabricated via electrospinning method by using 1% TCPs and CDs. The in vitro biocompatibility and osteoinductive properties of the scaffolds were examined by cytocompatibility and ALP assays. The results showed that PCL/PVA-TCP3-CD, heated egg shell-derived TCP, had significant proliferation and osteogenic differentiation compared to other scaffolds. Overall, these results suggest that adding CDs and utilization novel egg shell-derived calcium phosphate could be considered in fabrication of bioscaffold for the purpose of bone tissue engineering.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Research Deputy, Dental School, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences and Department of Tissue Engineering, School of Advanced Technologies in Medicine, Shahid Beheshti University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran.
